title: Coast Salish Formline: A Distinct Design System

steps.instruction: Establish the critical distinction: Coast Salish formline is not northern NW Coast formline. They are separate design systems.
steps.culturalContext: Coast Salish formline is a distinct design system from northern NW Coast formline (Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian). They are not variations of the same system. Northern formline uses the standardized ovoid and U-form system analyzed by art historian Bill Holm in his 1965 work 'Northwest Coast Indian Art: An Analysis of Form.' Coast Salish formline uses rounder, more oval forms, different positive/negative space relationships, and a different underlying grammar. Conflating the two systems erases the distinctiveness of both and misrepresents two independent design traditions. This distinction must be explicit in all content engaging with either system.

steps.instruction: Learn the northern NW Coast formline system (for contrast only — not for teaching as Coast Salish).
steps.culturalContext: The northern formline system — Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian — is characterized by four primary elements: the ovoid (a rounded rectangle with corners, often used for eye sockets and joints), the U-form (a curved U-shape filling secondary space), the S-curve (a dynamic diagonal element), and specific rules about how these elements connect at joints and flow between primary and secondary forms. It is a highly codified system with strict internal rules. Bill Holm's analysis made these rules explicit for Western scholarship, but the system was developed over millennia by northern NW Coast artists. Coast Salish formline is not this system — and teaching northern formline vocabulary as Coast Salish formline is an error that must be avoided.

steps.instruction: Learn the characteristics of Coast Salish formline design.
steps.culturalContext: Coast Salish formline: rounder and more freely oval forms rather than the structured ovoid of northern formline; circular and spiral compositions rather than flowing bilateral symmetry; a different relationship between positive (form) and negative (ground) space that uses the ground more actively as part of the composition. Coast Salish formline is best seen on spindle whorls — the circular disc creates a composition field that Coast Salish designers use very differently from how northern formline uses a rectangular or ovoid field. The circular field encourages radially symmetrical compositions where elements rotate around a center point.

steps.instruction: Study the spindle whorl as the primary canvas for Coast Salish formline design.
steps.culturalContext: The spindle whorl is a circular disc (typically 15-25 cm in diameter) that weights the drop spindle, giving it rotational momentum for spinning. In Coast Salish tradition, the whorl is also a primary canvas for formline design. Historic whorls carry compositions of recognizable figures — salmon, canoe paddlers, transformation figures — arranged within the circular field using Coast Salish formline vocabulary. Many whorls in museum collections carry compositions that may hold lineage associations, meaning they were made within a specific family tradition. Studying these whorls requires acknowledging that their specific design meanings may not be public knowledge, even when the whorls themselves are publicly displayed.

steps.instruction: Acknowledge design ownership: the boundary between general design principles (Level 1) and specific composition meanings (Level 2).
steps.culturalContext: Specific formline compositions may carry family meaning — they are not simply decorative. Studying Coast Salish formline means engaging with a living design tradition whose specific compositions belong to specific families and communities. General design principles — oval forms, circular composition structure, use of positive and negative space — are Level 1 educational content appropriate for general instruction. The meaning of specific compositions on specific objects is Level 2 contextually shared knowledge — always approach with acknowledgment that this design tradition belongs to Coast Salish families, and that specific meanings require community context to understand accurately.

steps.instruction: Recognize Coast Salish formline as a living tradition with contemporary practitioners.
steps.culturalContext: Coast Salish formline is a living tradition actively practiced and developed by contemporary Coast Salish artists. Artists including Susan Point (Musqueam) and Maynard Johnny Jr. (Kwantlen/Laich-kwil-tach) are among those developing the tradition today — extending the design vocabulary while maintaining the visual grammar of the Coast Salish formline system. This is not a museum tradition or an archaeological artifact — it is living cultural practice with contemporary practitioners whose work shapes how the tradition evolves. When referencing specific artists' work, acknowledge the individual artist and their nation. When referencing the tradition generally, acknowledge it as belonging to Coast Salish peoples (Musqueam, Squamish, Stó:lō, and other Coast Salish communities).
